issue_title: BasePathMapping deployment error: Invalid stage identifier specified
issue_body:
I have a SAM template deploying three resources: An ApiGateway, a Lambda Function and a ApiGateway BasePathMapping.

```
    AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
    Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
    Parameters:
      StageName:
        Type: String
    Resources:
      api:
        Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
        Properties:
          DefinitionBody: ...
          StageName: !Ref StageName
      apidomain:
        Type: AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping
        Properties:
          DomainName: mydomain.com
          RestApiId: !Ref api
          Stage: !Ref StageName
      lambda:
        Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
        Properties: ...
```

There is one parameter to the template called StageName, and this is used in two places:

1. The StageName for the ApiGateway
2. The Stage value for the BasePathMapping

When this stack is deployed, Cloud Formation deploys things in a strange order. It deploys the Api Gateway before the BasePathMapping, which you would expect, but deploying the ApiGateway stage is a separate step. It tries to deploy the BasePathMapping before the ApiGateway Stage, which results in the error:

> Invalid stage identifier specified

This is totally underdtandable given it has not been created yet.

I have tried using the DependsOn property on the BasePathMapping resource to force it to get created after the ApiGateway. It does, but not after the ApiGateway stage.

Does anyone know a way around this? It feels like a bug with the AWS::Serverless::Api resource type. It clearly needs to create a Gateway and a Stage, but the should be done sequentially not not allow the BasePathMapping to be created in between.

**Work arounds I don't like include:**

1. A two stage deployment. First without the BasePathMapping, then with it

issue_title: DependsOn AWS::Serverless:Api
issue_body:
# Background

I try deploying a serverless application which has a couple of Lambdas, an API gateway and I want to lock it behind an API key.

When sending the definition to cloudformation with `aws cloudformation deploy` I notice that `DependsOn` does wait on the `RestApi` resource to be ready, but it does not wait on the `AWS:ApiGateway::Deployment` resource which also is created by SAM (but transparently).

My SAM.yaml for reference:

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: 'AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31'
Description: Some API key protected serverless application
Parameters:
  targetStage:
    Description: Define stage to which Lambdas/API Gateways should be deployed.
    Type: String
    Default: dev
    AllowedValues:
      - dev
      - test
      - prod
    ConstraintDescription: Only stages dev, test, prod are allowed
Globals:
  Function:
    Runtime: nodejs6.10
    MemorySize: 128
    Timeout: 6
Resources:
  createFunction:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Function'
    Properties:
      Handler: create.handler
      CodeUri: ../dist/deployment_package.zip
      FunctionName: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-createCertificate-${targetStage}"
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
      Description: Create or overwrite an IoT certificate with given params.
      Role: 'arn:aws:iam::1234:role/my-role'
  deleteFunction:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Function'
    Properties:
      Handler: delete.handler
      CodeUri: ../dist/deployment_package.zip
      FunctionName: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-deleteCertificate-${targetStage}"
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
      Description: Delete an IoT certificate with given params.
      Role: 'arn:aws:iam::1234:role/my-role'
  restApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      StageName: !Ref targetStage
      DefinitionBody:
        swagger: "2.0"
        info:
          version: "2017-11-09T13:59:26Z"
          title: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-api-${targetStage}"
        basePath: !Sub "/${targetStage}"
        schemes:
          - "https"
        paths:
          /certificate:
            post:
              responses: {}
              security:
              - api_key: []
              x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
                uri: !Sub "arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${createFunction.Arn}/invocations"
                passthroughBehavior: "when_no_match"
                httpMethod: "POST"
                type: "aws_proxy"
            delete:
              responses: {}
              security:
              - api_key: []
              x-amazon-apigateway-integration:
                uri: !Sub "arn:aws:apigateway:${AWS::Region}:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/${deleteFunction.Arn}/invocations"
                passthroughBehavior: "when_no_match"
                httpMethod: "POST"
                type: "aws_proxy"
        securityDefinitions:
          api_key:
            type: "apiKey"
            name: "x-api-key"
            in: "header"
        x-amazon-apigateway-binary-media-types:
          - "application/octet-stream"
  apiKey:
    Type: "AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey"
    DependsOn:
      - restApi
    Properties:
      Name: !Sub "${AWS::StackName}-apiKey-${targetStage}"
      Description: !Sub "API key for: ${AWS::StackName} / ${targetStage}"
      Enabled: "true"
      StageKeys:
        - RestApiId: !Ref restApi
          StageName: !Ref targetStage
```

# Expected behaviour

`DependsOn` used to wait on a `AWS::Serverless:Api` should wait on all resources created by SAM for the API.

# Actual behaviour

This causes CloudFormation to throw a `CREATE_FAILED | AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey | apiKey | Invalid stage identifier specified` error during deployment, since the stage which I want to associate with the API key does not exist yet and I have no means of referencing the (future) stage or referencing the `AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment` in the `DependsOn`.

# Thoughts

The only workaround I can think of for now is to build all `AWS::Serverless::Api` component resources (RestApi, Deployment, ApiKey) by hand and use Depends on the respective resource identifiers.

issue_title: Inject StageVariable into API resource definition that uses a Swagger file
issue_body:
When using a Swagger file to define an API, users have to explicitly specify `Stage Variables` to hold the Lambda function ARN and use the stage variable in their Swagger template to specify the integration. This is not intuitive.

We could simplify this process by automatically injecting the Stage Variable when users specify an API Event source to their Lambda Function

When customers specify a template like this:
```
LambdaFunction:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
  Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Events:
         GetApi:
             Type: API
              Properties:
                  Path: /
                  Method: GET
                  RestApiId: !Ref MyApiResource

MyApiResource:
   Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
   Properties:
        DefinitionUri: s3://bucket/myswagger.yaml
```

we could inject a StageVariable to the API resource where variable name is equal to the function resource's logical name and value equal to the function's ARN

```
MyApiResource:
   Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
   Properties:
        DefinitionUri: s3://bucket/myswagger.yaml
        Variables:
             LambdaFunction: !Ref LambdaFunction
```

issue_title: Feature request: Allow discovery of verification addresses/ports via DNS SRV
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 278457504, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20boulder', 'name': 'area: boulder', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 200596079, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20ui%20/%20ux', 'name': 'area: ui / ux', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954789, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '00ccff', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
Seems like a sensible solution to #2697 and similar without compromising security would be to allow discovery of the port via DNS SRV record. Letsencrypt could query a record like

```
_letsencrypt._tcp IN SRV 0 0 8080 10.10.10.10
```

on the domain and forward verification requests to the resulting address/port combination. This has the same benefits as DNS verification but without the drawback of requiring dynamic updates.

This would also mean users could easily centralise the management of their certificates if they wish, by referencing a central cert server.

issue_title: "scroll" keyword has a vague name and lacks documentation
issue_body:
When trying to understand how to best use `helpers.scan` I was very puzzled by the `scroll` keyword. For quick reference, here's the function signature:
```
scan(client, query=None, scroll=u'5m', raise_on_error=True, preserve_order=False, size=1000, 
request_timeout=None, clear_scroll=True, scroll_kwargs=None, **kwargs)
```

 I now understand that it has to do with how long minutes to keep the view open, but here's why I think it has some problems with the way it is right now:

1. **"Scroll" as a name doesn't tell us what the argument does.** Unless "scroll" is commonly understood to mean 'view time' in the search world, argument name itself could mean anything. A better name would be `view_time` or `consistant_view_time` or really anything that implies that the argument is a time parameter.

2. **The default parameter doesn't help.** The "m" in "5m" could just as well mean "millions" It doesn't allude to "minutes" and we can't infer it since other units aren't offered in the documentation. Is "h" acceptable for hours? How about "s". How granular can we get? Is "1.53m" acceptable? If only minutes are acceptable, then why is the argument a `str` instead of `int`

3. **The documentation is terse**. Again, now that I know scroll is related to time, when reading
>Specify how long a consistent view of the index should be maintained for scrolled search

it's obvious what it's talking about, but if you don't know that this is a time parameter then "long" could very well be interpreted as having something to do with the amount of results. That the `size` is the batch size while `scroll` is the total number of results.

Though I think changing the name of the parameter and an expansion of the documentation about acceptable params would be best, really, alluding to time in any point would make this parameter a great deal more understandable

issue_title: Using RequestsHttpConnection in 5.x code with proxies doesn't work
issue_body:
In our environment we use proxy servers. In addition we use AWS Elasticsearch domains hence the need to use the requests connection class with the custom auth.

We have found that proxies are not being honored in the 5.x code, and tracked it down to the change made in #481. Per the guidance issued by the requests authors in https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3626, I believe the code should add a call to merge_environment_settings() in order to bring in the proxy settings. e.g. in perform_request() do:
  
```python
request = requests.Request(method=method, url=url, data=body)
prepared_request = self.session.prepare_request(request)
settings = self.session.merge_environment_settings(prepared_request.url, {}, None, None, None)
send_kwargs = {'timeout': timeout or self.timeout}
send_kwargs.update(settings)
response = self.session.send(prepared_request, **send_kwargs)
```

Another approach to take is to just go back to not using prepared requests. The reason I think you want the prepared_request is to be able to get to the `path_url` attr of the prepared request when an exception is raised (the path being used in trace logging). If we could get the path without needing to construct an entire prepared_request then we can simplify the above down to one line again.

issue_title: Accidentally Deleting Several Cells Using 'X'
issue_body:
Hello!
I love the Jupyter Notebook and I use it extensively in my research. However, there is one feature that has been nagging me for a while.

My browser (Chrome on MacOS) sends several keydown events when I'm using the keyboard shortcuts in the notebook. Holding the S button will perform several saves, and holding the X button for 1 - 2 seconds will remove all the cells in the notebook (holding D does the same but slower). 

I don't know if is because my fingers are too fat, that I have bad coordination or both... But I accidentally delete cells from time to time (mainly because X is so close to the Cmd-button). Since there is only one undo step, the results can be devastating.  

Is there a solution to this that I have missed? 

Otherwise I suggest adding a popup, or implementing one of the approaches outlined in the answers here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7686197/how-can-i-avoid-autorepeated-keydown-events-in-javascript

I think this is the part that causes the problem:
https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/notebook/js/keyboardmanager.js#L130

issue_title: Richer Exporting ("Print Preview" and "Download as…") interface
issue_body:
Some of the nicer features of nbconvert are currently only easily available on the command line. Those more comfortable in the browser can indirectly customise their exports using the `jupyter_config` and `jupyter_notebook_config` files, but that only picks up changes if you restart the server.

One suggested solution is to have a richer version of the "Download as…" that then actually calls out to nbconvert using an API (rather than what it's currently doing which is seemingly more complicated than just running a subprocess with `nbconvert` calls). @Carreau can hopefully say more about this both what the current mechanism is and how the API would work.

Additionally, rather than just "Download as…" being customisable, it would be useful to have a customisable version of "Print Preview". That would include a live preview, which in Jupyter lab could be updated in real-time (or as close to realtime as is possible) @fperez @bollwyvl. 

This stems from the January 17, 2017 Jupyter dev meeting discussion.

issue_title: Long tuples in map reduce
issue_body:
Hello!

I have started to use luigi for hadoop streaming. Jobs with "key\tvalue" records work fine. But when I started to use "key\tvalue1\tvalue2..." tuples, reduce function receives value1 only. Reason is in ```hadoop.JobTask._reduce_input``` function:

```
def _reduce_input(self, inputs, reducer, final=NotImplemented):
        """
        Iterate over input, collect values with the same key, and call the reducer for each unique key.
        """
        for key, values in groupby(inputs, key=lambda x: self.internal_serialize(x[0])):
            for output in reducer(self.deserialize(key), (v[1] for v in values)):
                yield output
        if final != NotImplemented:
            for output in final():
                yield output
        self._flush_batch_incr_counter()
```

Should it be ```v[1:]``` instead of ```v[1]```?
Is it bug or feature?

I can always redefine this function in subclass, but it looks like a bug.

issue_title: Avoid exception handling for PostgresTarget.create_marker_table() with "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS"
issue_body:
I think it would be more elegant to add `IF NOT EXISTS` in the query for creating the `marker_table` in `luigi.contrib.postgres` module, instead of dealing with an exception to handle the case where the table already exists.

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS {marker_table} {
update_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
target_table TEXT,
inserted TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW())
```

`IF NOT EXISTS` is available since postgres 9.1 (released more than 5 years ago) and it seems that it is also available in many RDBMS.

So if the method should be rewrote I guess it would be as follows:

```python
    def create_marker_table(self):
        """
        Create marker table if it doesn't exist.

        Using a separate connection since the transaction might have to be reset.
        """
        connection = self.connect()
        connection.autocommit = True
        cursor = connection.cursor()
        if self.use_db_timestamps:
            sql = """ CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS {marker_table} (
                      update_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                      target_table TEXT,
                      inserted TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW())
                """.format(marker_table=self.marker_table)
        else:
            sql = """ CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS {marker_table} (
                      update_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                      target_table TEXT,
                      inserted TIMESTAMP);
                  """.format(marker_table=self.marker_table)
        try:
            cursor.execute(sql)
        except psycopg2.ProgrammingError as e:
                # in case there is another exception, i.e. other than a duplicated table name, raise
                raise
        connection.close()
```

Again I am not experienced enough to make a PR, so if any good soul can manage to do it...

Thanks

issue_title: Multiple encoded outputs into a single classification network
issue_body:
![networksketch](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29553460/27308621-9efe609c-551d-11e7-81c7-0cbfc898f266.png)

I have multiple data sets, where data sets are distinguished by feature length of a feature vector.
Regardless input feature size, I would like the network to be able to classify one of the two classes given the input. Hence, I would like to train an encoder for each data set (will train n encoders for n datasets), but pass the encoded outputs from all encoders to a single classification network since the encoded outputs are of the same dimension. See attached image for the network sketch.

I have code written below so far, 
1. but I am not really sure if the current way of setting up the regression is valid or need to use merge with some sort of 'mean' to average classification results from different data sets.
2. I am also having trouble figuring out how to perform training given the situation.

Any help will be greatly appreciated. 

```
import h5py
import numpy as np
import tflearn

STEPS_PER_EPOCH = 10
NUM_CLASSES = 2

train_Xs = []
train_Ys = []
batch_sizes = []
encodings = []

encode_hidden_1 = 500
classify_hidden_1 = 500
classify_hidden_2 = 100
for trainDataset in trainDatasets:
    train_file = h5py.File(trainDataset,'r')
    train_X = np.array(train_file['features'])
    train_Y = np.array(train_file['label'])
    train_Xs(train_X) # number of samples x number of features
    train_Ys(train_Y) # number of samples x 2 (two classes)
    nb_samples = train_X.shape[0]
    nb_features = train_X.shape[1]
    batch_size = int(nb_samples/STEPS_PER_EPOCH) 
    # batch size is determined by the number of samples in each dataset
    
    encoder = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None, nb_features])
    encoder = tflearn.fully_connected(encoder, encode_hidden_1)
    encodings.append(encoder)   

classifiers_1 = []
classifiers_2 = []
softmax_outputs = []
for encoding in encodings:
    classifier1 = tflearn.fully_connected(encoding, classify_hidden_1, activation='relu')
    classifiers_1.append(classifier1)
    classifier2 = tflearn.fully_connected(classifiers1, classify_hidden_2, activation='relu')
    classifiers_2.append(classifier2)
    softmax = tflearn.fully_connected(classifier2, 2, activation='softmax')
    softmax_outputs.append(softmax)

network = regression(softmax_outputs, optimizer = 'momentum', loss='categorical_crossentropy', learning_rate=0.1)

```

issue_title: Losing in leader election clears need_rewind flag
issue_body:
``process_unhealthy_cluster()` does:

``` python
    need_rewind = bool(self.cluster.failover) or self.patroni.nofailover
```

and then `Postgresql.follow()` does:

``` python
if need_rewind is not None:
    self._need_rewind = need_rewind
```

This looks incorrect to me. I assume tacking on an `or None` default onto there is what was intended. I'll continue my refactoring based on this assumption, but wanted to double check as the need_rewind logic is hard to follow.

issue_title: Status code 204 should be allowed to return headers metadata
issue_body: Currently connexion does not provide a way to return headers with metadata on [204 HTTP response codes](https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/produces.py#L123-L125) from endpoints. On my perspective it should be allowed as described in the [RFC-2616 section 10.2.5](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html) of HTTP/1.1.

issue_title: [Z3py] Empty Sum/Product in ForAll raises exception
issue_body:
The following raises "Z3Exception: Z3 expression expected":
>> s=Solver()
>> t=Const('t',IntSort())
>> s.add( ForAll([t], Sum([])==0) )

No exception is raised if the empty sum is part of a larger expression, or if variables appear in the expression -- i.e. no exception is raised by
>> s.add( ForAll([t], Not( Sum([])==0 )) )
or 
>> s.add( ForAll([t],  Sum([])==t ) )
The empty product Product([]) gives the same results; however there is no corresponding problem with empty conjunctions or disjunctions.

issue_title: Sub app middlewares are called before main app,
issue_body:
## Long story short

According to http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web.html#nested-applications,
`[I]f URL is '/admin/something' middlewares from [main application] are applied first and [sub application] middlewares are the next in the call chain.` 

It turns out it's actually the other way.

## Steps to reproduce

Run this code saved as foo.py:
```python
#!/usr/bin/python3 -tt

import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web

async def middleware1(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware1")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler
    
async def middleware2(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware2")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler

async def middleware3(app, next_handler):

    async def handler(request):
        print("middleware3")
        return await next_handler(request)

    return handler

app = aiohttp.web.Application(
    middlewares=[middleware1])

subapp1 = aiohttp.web.Application(middlewares=[middleware2])
app.add_subapp("/foo", subapp1)

subapp2 = aiohttp.web.Application(middlewares=[middleware3])
subapp1.add_subapp("/foo/bar", subapp2)

aiohttp.web.run_app(app, host='127.0.0.1', port=4096)
```

And the run `wget http://127.0.0.1:4096/foo/bar`.

### Current Result:
```
$ python3 foo.py
======== Running on http://127.0.0.1:4096 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
middleware3
middleware2
middleware1
```

### Expected output:
```
$ python3 foo.py
======== Running on http://127.0.0.1:4096 ========
(Press CTRL+C to quit)
middleware1
middleware2
middleware3
```

## Your environment

Ubuntu Xenial (python 3.5.2) with aiohttp 2.0.7

issue_title: Force keys to str instances
issue_body:
Right now a key type is dark corner: it could be str, istr or something other.
For sake of further optimizations it should be `str` always.

Passing `istr` as key to any miltidict API is still allowed and encouraged: it saves execution time by getting rid of `s.title()` conversion.

But `istr` type is ambiguous by it nature: we can define equality for `str` and `istr` by comparing their *canonical* representations but ordering is cumbersome.
Ordering by canonical reprs leads to very tricky errors, the same for *original* reprs.
Also CPython is very optimized for `str` but not classes derived from it.

I believe the change doesn't touch our users but aiohttp might get speedup on implementing the issue.

issue_title: Multidicts does not preserve order on update
issue_body:
When updating existing key in multidict, it is moved to the end (original order is not preserved):
```
>>> d = CIMultiDict()
>>> d['1'] = '2'
>>> d['3'] = '4'
>>> d
<CIMultiDict('1': '2', '3': '4')>
>>> d['1'] = '10'
>>> d
<CIMultiDict('3': '4', '1': '10')>
```
In above example, guarantee of preserving insertion order should leave existing key `'1'` on its original first position.

issue_title: Consider allowing PeerPoolSubscribers to specify types of messages they're not interested in
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Some PeerPoolSubscribers (e.g. `LightPeerChain`) are only meant to handle a subset of msg types, so it is rather wasteful to have `PeerPool` deliver all messages to their queues.

### How can it be fixed

We could make it possible for `PeerPoolSubscribers` to specify what types of msgs they aren't interested in, so that they never get those. Or they could specify the types of msgs they are interested in, but the problem with that is we need to remember to update the list if/when they're extended to handle more msg types

issue_title: Add JSON-RPC over IPC process to Trinity
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

We need to spin up the JSON-RPC server over IPC whenever we are running a chain.

### How can it be fixed

This should be done as it's own process (`multiprocessing.Process`).

We should *loosely* mirror the flags that `parity/go-ethereum` use (I think I like the ones from parity more):

- `--no-ipc` to disable the server entirely
- `--ipc-apis` comma separated string of the APIs to be exposed (currently we only have `eth`)
- `--ipc-path` user supplied path for where the IPC socket should be located.  Default to `$XDG_DATA_HOME/trinity/<chain-identifier>/jsonrpc.ipc`

There will be some complexity and maybe dependency ordering as we figure out how to expose the chain database across multiple processes.

issue_title: Filename collision in multiple re-import calls
issue_body:
In processes which have multiple `basic:file` input fields and use `re-import` function, the following scenario can occur. Suppose that a user has two different files with the same file name stored in different directories. The user then calls a process with two input `basic:file` fields, providing the two aforementioned files. If the first uploaded file is not renamed in the process before the second re-import call, the first file is overwritten by the second file during the second re-import call. Although the process operates with two different `basic:file` input fields, they both point to the same file in the `data` directory.

Two examples of affected processes are `upload-expression` and `upload-fastq-paired`. 

It is unwise to expect that issue will be taken care of manually in each process. A general solution is needed.

issue_title: Consider restoring support for Python 2.7
issue_body:
Would you consider restoring support for Python 2.7?
Some of us are unable to upgrade to Python 3 just yet.

- Django 1.8 is the previous LTS and supported until at least April 2018.
- Django 1.10 is end-of-life December 2017.
- Django 1.11 is the current LTS and supported until at least April 2020.
- Django 2.0 drops support for Python 2.

Django 1.11 is the last version to support Python 2.7 and there is no version of django-axes that supports both of these.

issue_title: Global settings for access mixins
issue_body:
In a project, we usually have a global way of handling accesses in views.
The problem with access current access mixins, we need to configure *raise_exception*, *redirect_unanthenticated_users* and other mixins settings in the view class.
If I'm not wrong, their is two way of having the same settings on all views:

  + create our own AccessMixins with those attribute set to our required values.
  + set those settings in each views.

In one of my project, I had a huge number of views I needed to re-configure access for.

I think we need to have some globals settings that should be set to configure access mixins default attribute values. For exemple:

```python
# file: settings.py

BRACES = {
    'access_raise_exception': True,
    'redirect_unauthenticated_users': True,
}
```

issue_title: Difflib and python-Levenshtein give different ratios in some cases
issue_body:
This is probably known and accepted, but since it's not mentioned in the docs I'll raise this anyway. In certain edge cases, difflib and python-Levenshtein give different ratios.
E.g.:
```
>>> from fuzzywuzzy import fuzz, StringMatcher
>>> import difflib

#As long as python-Levenshtein is available, that will be used for the following:
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "aaaaab")
67

#Switch to difflib:
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher = difflib.SequenceMatcher
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "aaaaab")
33
```

I don't know how python-Levenshtein works, but difflib first chooses the left-most longest block in the first sequence that matches any block in the second sequence. Then among the longest matches in the second sequence to this chosen block, it will choose the left-most.

What this means is that it is forced to choose the first "ab" in the first string, and then matches it to the only "ab" in the second, at the end. This means it cannot recurse either to the left or the right to look for more matching blocks, since this match is at the very beginning of the first string and at the very end of the second. So the ratio calculated is 33, since it has matched one third (4/12) of the total characters.

I'm assuming that in python-Levenshtein, it makes a decision about which matches to choose based not just on maximality and left-most-ness, but also on whether the pair of matches chosen allows subsequent recursion. This is because it scores 67, so must have matched eight of 12 characters, i.e. it must have matched "ab" and then two subsequent "a"s in each sequence, meaning it must have chosen the last "ab" in the first string. Either this or it can recurse to opposite sides in the two sequences but that surely isn't happening.

To show this, if we change the second sequence to "abaaaa", difflib will also score 67 (since it matches the first two characters of each sequence then recurses to the right). See as follows:

```
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "abaaaa")
67

#And switching pack to python-Levenshtein, no change:
>>> fuzz.SequenceMatcher = fuzzywuzzy.StringMatcher.StringMatcher
>>> fuzz.ratio("ababab", "abaaaa")
67
```

issue_title: Can't login from behind proxy
issue_body:
When the `pycrunch.elements.ElementResponseHandler.status_401` method resends the original request (https://github.com/Crunch-io/pycrunch/blob/9f78f3e567902b71ebc06fc4a2de4fb97d5cdc4a/pycrunch/elements.py#L226) it does so without considering proxy information, which means it can't connect.

The proxy information isn't stored as part of the original request, so just using `self.session.send` won't automatically work unless there's a way to attach that information to every call to `.send`, for example by storing on the session. Note that all calls to `.send` should also consider environment information too, as done by `requests.Session.request`: https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/requests/sessions.py#L518

Here's an example in a `pdb` session:
```
(Pdb) self.session.send(r.request)                                          
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:Starting new HTTPS connection (6): app.crunch.io
# times out
^C*** KeyboardInterrupt: 
(Pdb) self.session.send(r.request, proxies=dict(http='<redacted>', https='<redacted>'))
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:Resetting dropped connection: app.crunch.io
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:Starting new HTTPS connection (3): app.crunch.io
DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:https://app.crunch.io:443 "GET /api/ HTTP/1.1" 200 447
<Response [200]>
```

Possible solutions:

1. Allow extra kwargs to be passed through to the underlying session in pycrunch.connect
2. Consider calling `session.merge_environment_settings` with some sensible defaults and passing into `self.session.send`

issue_title: Serializing modules can be slow
issue_body:
Here is an analysis from a colleague:

### Quote

The speed-up for us seems to be coming from the fact that pickling modules takes a long time:
```
In [25]: %timeit cloudpickle.dumps(numpy, -1)
100 loops, best of 3: 3.03 ms per loop
```
It looks like ``_find_module()`` will use ``imp.find_module()`` which traverses ``sys.path`` to look for things that look like numpy.  In our environment, sys.path tends to be long and our filesystems tend to be slow, hence the 3.03 ms. 

```python
    def save_module(self, obj):
        """
        Save a module as an import
        """
        mod_name = obj.__name__
        # If module is successfully found then it is not a dynamically created module
        try:
            _find_module(mod_name)     # EXPENSIVE!!!!!
            is_dynamic = False
        except ImportError:
            is_dynamic = True

        self.modules.add(obj)
        if is_dynamic:
            self.save_reduce(dynamic_subimport, (obj.__name__, vars(obj)), obj=obj)
        else:
            self.save_reduce(subimport, (obj.__name__,), obj=obj)
    dispatch[types.ModuleType] = save_module
```
So it looks like cloudpickle is trying to allow for "dynamically created modules".  If it didn't try to be this flexible, then the entire function should just be
```python
self.save_reduce(subimport, (obj.__name__,), obj=obj)
```
So the danger is if people are using "dynamically created modules", which we don't tend to do.

Maybe an easy way out is to check if ``obj.__file__`` exists (the attribute, not the file).  If it does, then immediately assume that is_dynamic=False.

Fwiw, I think we're pickling ``numpy`` because we're pickling functions that refer to ``numpy``.  Not positive though.

issue_title: Bread Crumbs showing data from other uWSGI requests
issue_body: Hi Sentry Team, We are running a Django application, with a uWSGI config of the following (Irrelevant data snipped): ``` [uwsgi] project = gapi-layer-drf base = /var/www home = /opt/lib/virtualenvs/%(project) chdir = /var/www/gapi-layer-drf/gapi pythonpath = /var/www/gapi-layer-drf/gapi module = gapi.wsgi plugins-dir = /opt/uwsgi/uwsgi-2.0.13.1 plugins = python35 uid = www-data gid = www-data harakiri = 20 # thunder-lock is basically necessary with threading. Helps deal with the "thundering herd" problem. thunder-lock = true # memory-report is necessary if doing stats. Proper reporting of data. memory-report = true logto = /var/log/uwsgi/%(project).log logfile-chown = www-data master = true processes = 5 threads = 5 # Hush uWSGI from IOError, SIGPIPE, etc. ignore-sigpipe = true ignore-write-errors = true disable-write-exception = true # Setup a socket, which is more secure and offers better performance than a network port. socket = /tmp/%(project).sock chmod-socket = 666 chown-socket = www-data # Use vacuum to clean up when the service is stopped. vacuum = true ``` We are using Django Rest Framework and a custom Exception Handler. Essentially, any exception is captured, transformed for the response, but also delivered to Sentry via the standard `logging` module. We are using Raven `5.32` The Bread Crumbs seem to work well for the most part, and the tail of the crumbs indeed shows the appropriate request that caused the exception. For example. Here's the tail end of a crumb, with the exception/time: Then, above that, I have more crumbs, that are generally hidden (but not always). The primary thing to note is the **substantial time difference** between the previous crumbs and these ones. Essentially, they are not part of the same request-response cycle (and knowing our system, I can confirm that. We'd never hit a `booking` while hitting the `Flight Control` backend within a single request) So, quite an interesting bit for us. It's not necessarily an issue, but it might confuse our developers (or even myself in a pre-coffee state) what's going on with this request. Anything else I can provide, please let me know. Happy to help. Thank you!

issue_title: Missing error report while replication error occures
issue_body:
Hello, I use ```tarantool-python``` for the purposes of Tarantool cluster management. I've faced with a misleading (from my point of view) behavior when I set ```replication_source``` for a particular Tarantool node.

```python
In [21]: import tarantool

In [22]: c = tarantool.connect('ops-4.dev.search.ru', 33018)

In [23]: c.authenticate('id', 'id_pass')
Out[23]: 
null
...

In [24]: r = c.eval("return box.cfg{ replication_source = { 'id:id_pass@ops-7.dev.search.ru:33018' } }")

In [25]: r.strerror
Out[25]: ('ER_OK', 'OK')

In [26]: r.data
Out[26]: []
```

But in Tarantool logs I see that the error actually happened:
```
Jul 13 19:19:03 ops-4 ltime[1]: main/127/iproto C> starting replication from ops-7.dev.search.ru:33018
Jul 13 19:19:03 ops-4 ltime[1]: main/127/iproto I> set 'replication_source' configuration option to ["id:id_pass@ops-7.dev.search.ru:33018"]
Jul 13 19:19:03 ops-4 ltime[1]: main/128/applier/ops-7.dev.search I> connected to 1.6.8 at 10.241.232.31:33018

```

Since no error was returned, could anyone please explain, if there are any convenient methods in Python API to check that replication really works.

issue_title: client.send_read_acknowldge failing for channels
issue_body:
<!--
0. The library is Python 3.x, not Python 2.x.
1. If you're posting an issue, make sure it's a bug in the library, not in your code.
2. If you're posting a question, make sure you have read and tried enough things first.
3. Show as much information as possible, including your failed attempts, and the full console output (to include the whole traceback with line numbers).
4. Good looking issues are a lot more appealing. If you need help check out https://guides.github.com/features/mastering-markdown/.

You may also want to watch "How (not) to ask a technical question" over https://youtu.be/53zkBvL4ZB4
-->
Offending code:
```
        entity = self.get_input_entity(entity)
        if entity == InputPeerChannel:
            return self(channels.ReadHistoryRequest(entity, max_id=max_id))
        else:
            return self(messages.ReadHistoryRequest(entity, max_id=max_id))
```
Fix that works for me:
```
        entity = self.get_input_entity(entity)
        if isinstance(entity, InputPeerChannel):
            return self(channels.ReadHistoryRequest(entity, max_id=max_id))
        else:
            return self(messages.ReadHistoryRequest(entity, max_id=max_id))
```

issue_title: ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING behaviour assumes existence of an 'id' field
issue_body:
Thanks for this package, it looks like it could be very useful!

I've hit an issue with the `DO NOTHING` handling -- it assumes that a field called `id` exists on the model, though this isn't always the case.

``` python
class Foo(PostgresModel):
    user = models.OneToOneField(User, primary_key=True)
```

This results in a table with a primary key field called `user_id`,

It looks like the error stems from the way that the returning data is captured in the compilers' [`_rewrite_insert_nothing`](https://github.com/SectorLabs/django-postgres-extra/blob/master/psqlextra/compiler.py#L158) method which uses a literal `id` in the query construction.

We're using the module for a tool we wrote called apkid, so we have a vested interest in maintaining the module: https://github.com/rednaga/APKiD

issue_title: Disable Rollback on Failure
issue_body:
Would it be possible to add in an option to disable rollback on failure for stack debugging? This is useful if things don't deploy correctly and you need to investigate (Especially instances). You currently have it as OnFailure='DELETE'  in the code, there is an additional option for Rollback which allows you to leave things in place. However you cannot specify both. 

DisableRollback='True',

Maybe it could be toggled and linked to something like a --debug switch or by specifying debug in the configuration file at the top with the timeouts?

Jonathan

issue_title: Simple round-trip fails to reconstruct object of locally-defined class
issue_body:
The following unit test, inside a function, triggers the first assertion (or will trigger the second if the 1st is removed)

    def test_json_stuff(self):
        import jsonpickle
        class SomeObject(object):
             pass
        object_original = SomeObject()
        object_clone = jsonpickle.decode(jsonpickle.encode(object_original))
        self.assertFalse(isinstance(object_clone, type(dict())))
        self.assert_(isinstance(object_clone, SomeObject))

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 248, in test_json_stuff
    self.assertFalse(isinstance(object_clone, type(dict())))
AssertionError


Basically it's returning the plain dict instead of reconstructing the object as expected. However, if the definition of the 'SomeObject' class is moved to file scope, it works.

The worst bit about this is not the limitation, but the silent failure and the returning of the dict instead of raising an exception.

issue_title: message buffer peak usage - verses "normality" & running out of buffers.
issue_body:
This is more of a question about openthreads message buffer implementation, and how buffer management is done.  It seems the default message buffer count is minimal at best and possibly far to small on the NCP device.

I have what I believe to be a simple test case -  NCP + Border Router  + One joining device - Android APP  - During the joining process, the peak buffer usage is 31 (of 40) buffers in use (75% are busy) leaving only 9 buffers, at 5 minutes into the process about 50% of the buffers are still busy - it takes a full 7 minutes to return to what I would call a normal level of activity (ie: 2 busy, 38 free)

it is important to note that in my example test network there is zero "application traffic" - it is only thread network self induced activity.

My question is along these lines - sadly it is not a distinct single question

* For the NCP - is the "40 buffers" reasonable?  Should this be larger?
* Or is this really small bare (spec) minimum, but not enough to be practical?

* If the NCP is a router, and in this example it is AND if there where other devices on the network (ie: sleep devices) and routing between devices - the other network traffic would there would be, and there is an amount of other traffic (there would be several) or other activity (ie: messages routed up stream to the public internet or to the local in-home network)) the NCP will probably run out of buffers during the commissioning process.

Below is some detail and background (data) to my question. 

-Duane.

COMMISSIONING TIME LINE
===================

At time 0 - the NCP is reset, and is not part of a network.

Within about 10 to 15 seconds - I have used the WEB browser and created a network, and I have used the Android App to scan a barcode to commission a new device.

IMPORTANT - Before hand - 

In the "message.cpp" file - where messages are allocated & freed, insert a bit of code to print out the total number of FREE messages each time a message is allocated or free. It is this number we are tracking from here on out.

In this simple use case - the result is a peak usage of 31 of the 40 (75%) leaving 9 free buffers.

If there is ANY other activity (ie:  The NCP might be a router that needs to forward messages) - it often runs out of messages.

Commissioning completes in about 1 minute 
Actual time in my test case is timestamp = 74 seconds.
But I'll use the 1 minute as a nice round number for this discussion.

I'm not - purposefully stopping the android app - I am being a "an average user who would not know to do this"

At +2 minutes (120 seconds)  - there are 18 to 19 free messages (22 are in use)
(there has been a little activity, a few messages allocated and freed)

At +3 minutes (180 seconds) - same: 18 to 19 free messages
(there has been a little activity, a few messages allocated and freed)

Note: at 234 seconds - the NCP announces "commissioner in active" caused by a system level commissioning timeout - I did not 'cancel' the process.

At +4 minutes (240 seconds) -  same: 18 to 19 free messages (20 free)

MOST of these messages are, and have been stuck/lingering in the coap response queue.

At +5 minutes (300 seconds) - the curve begins to bend... 22 free messages (50%)

At +6 minutes (360 seconds) - 31 free messages 
  20 seconds later - there are 32 free messages

At +7 minutes (420 seconds) - 37 free messages - (3 busy messages)

In my example network - a single sample mtd end device does not transmit, it emulates a binary 1/0 output (think lightbulb) - it can locally be turned on/off - or it can remotely (via  coap) change state. But to be clear: There is ZERO application layer network traffic. The numbers here are the network self induced message buffer usage.

From here on out - there are always 36 to 37 free messages - and I don't think there's much to say.

SIMPLE LINUX TEST APP - Polling Devices
===========================

Taking the completed above network (4 devices)
   2 of the devices are "paired" - a temperature sensor and a thermostat, they talk to each other
   2 of the devices are simple actuator type devices (button/light type) they do not talk to each other

On a linux box - I run a simple test script that does the following:

Loop Forever
     For device A, B, C, D
         using "libcoap example app: "coap-client" fetch data from the device.
         NOTE: this is "coap" (non-secure, not secure)

         Using "ping6" - ping the device.
     Sleep 5 seconds

Depending upon what is going on  - this peaks at about 10 buffers in use.  Meaning - commissioning has the possibility of having problems.  I do understand that UDP is lossy - and retries should occur...  but this example test case is small, it seems the this might be very "borderline" at best. 

Possible Bug?
===================

Lastly - I think there is a bug - this is why I started this investigation - but I'm not sure yet.

After running my simple forever loop test (described above) several hours (12 or so), to overnight (24hrs, or over the weekend) - the devices often loose (leak?) a message buffer

I think it would be helpful - on the posix simulation - to introduce some level of "lossy ness" to the network channel.  Meaning today - the network SEND operation is nearly perfect, for simulation purposes - I think it would be very helpful to add a bit of "random loss"  - where in the transmission process maybe  10 to 20% of the packets transmitted are thrown away and not transmitted (simulating a transmission jammer) and likewise in the simulated receiver, 10 to 20% of the packets are also thrown away  (simulating a receiver jammer)

Then - have some means to run the test process overnight for a very long time in both MTD and FTD modes.

-END-

issue_title: Illegal Network Name causes crashes
issue_body:
This actually crashes wpantund, but has roots in the mis-behaving remote device.

Not sure where this should be filed, as it is an architectural decision.

To reproduce:

From the 'wpanctl' application, use the "scan" command to scan for networks.  The code in wpantund (dbus, specifically) assumes, rightly or wrongly, that the network name is a UTF8 string. 

the NCP transmits a beacon request.

In this scenario, the remote device answering the beacon request has a bug and is sending a bad or invalid string as the network name. That device should transmit: "OpenThread" - instead it transmits: "O\377\377\377\377hread" (Where: \377 = Octal for an 0xFF byte).  It looks like something wrote a 32bit "-1" value over the top of something it should not have.

This goes over the air with the "0xff" bytes in place of a portion of the network name, up through the NCP and finally into Wpantund - where an assertion triggers this error:
```
process 592: arguments to dbus_message_iter_append_basic() were incorrect, assertion "_dbus_check_is_valid_utf8 (*string_p)" failed in file ../../dbus/dbus-message.c line 2676.
```
of course this should be fixed in the transmitting device, but the robustness principle states either the NCP, or WPANTUND ... something, somewhere shall not die a horrible death as it is right now.


-Duane.

issue_title: Reify Task-level invalidation, and isolate on-disk artifacts
issue_body:
As Task implementations and inputs change, their behaviour can change drastically. To account for this, we should expand the identity of a Task to include more than just its name, and include the additional information in Target-level hashes.

See https://groups.google.com/d/topic/pants-devel/4M89vjY94hE/discussion

issue_title: The `-c` form of `--pants-config-files` does not work for options bootstrapping.
issue_body: I discovered this doing ad-hoc testing for #4587 via `./pants -c=pants.cache.ini test.pytest tests/python/pants_test/cache/:cache_setup`. The local cache was not being turned on. The `-c` short form of `--pants-config-files` was added in #4715 but the [`get_config_file_paths`](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/option/options_bootstrapper.py#L28-L55) method was not updated to account for this alternate form. Updating that method raises issues since a mild TODO now becomes much more prominent without a refactoring of the options parsing system to ensure we read the global `-c` and not a local one.

issue_title: The python backend's `ThriftBuilder` `CodeGenerator` includes transitive thrifts.
issue_body:
This is unlike, for example the `PythonAntlrBuilder` `CodeGenerator`.  This is problematic since PythonChroot already does a walk in `resolve`, thus adding each PythonThriftLibrary to the set of 'thrift' targets to be code-gened by the ThriftBuilder.  As a result, we get multiple distributions containing code for any thrift transitive dependencies.  The leaf targets are correctly invalidated, since their fingerprint matches with the distribution contents; however the roots contents do not align with their fingerprint since they include sources their fingerprint does not own.  This last bit leads to trampling of thrift depending on PYTHONPATH load order.

This was discovered in #2005 by @wyattanderson

issue_title: Ban target names containing `/` or `@`
issue_body:
`/` is confusing for obvious reasons. `@` is newly reserved for use with variant keys.

We should start by deprecating usage of these patterns (triggering a warning), and then turn the warning into an error.

issue_title: pants.ini [binaries] section no longer applies after move to bootstrap options
issue_body:
in #4846 I landed a change to move the binaries options to bootstrap options. this seems to have had the unintended side effect of making the `[binaries]` section no longer work in `pants.ini`:

```
ERROR] Invalid option 'path_by_id' under [binaries] in /Users/kwilson/dev/source/pants.ini
ERROR] Invalid option 'fetch_timeout_secs' under [binaries] in /Users/kwilson/dev/source/pants.ini
ERROR] Invalid option 'baseurls' under [binaries] in /Users/kwilson/dev/source/pants.ini
...

Exception message: Invalid config entries detected. See log for details on which entries to update or remove.
(Specify --no-verify-config to disable this check.)
```

flattening these as e.g.

```
[GLOBAL]
binaries_baseurls: ...
binaries_fetch_timeout_secs: ...
binaries_path_by_id: ...
```

works as expected tho. 

it's not clear to me if this is a violation of the deprecation policy, but I'm also not sure how we can more cleanly migrate these options to bootstrap. posting here mainly for posterity but also to raise a discussion on the broader issue.

issue_title: JIRAError does not correctly display full error information
issue_body:
There seems to be a bug in JIRAError.__str__

        if self.response and hasattr(self.response, 'text'):
            t += "\n\tresponse text = " % self.response.headers

https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/master/jira/utils.py#L161

1. This checks for the `text` attribute but then uses the `headers` attribute (cut-and-paste error from the lines above?)
2. The response object is "Truthy" based upon the HTTP response, so in the case of a HTTP error it is actually "False" so the conditional check fails.
3. Which is just as well, because once you fix this you'll see that the format string is invalid and causes an exception because it is missing a %s at the end

:-)

I'll submit a pull request to fix this up shortly.

issue_title: 'MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings' has no attribute 'n_processes'
issue_body:
I'm trying to extract features using tsfresh package and extract_features() function. I'm trying to set `MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings` . 

tsfresh Version: 0.4.0.post0.dev1+ng19fa136

However, I get the following error:

`AttributeError:` type object 'MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings' has no attribute 'n_processes'`

Code:
```
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

column_names = ['time_series1', 'time_series2','time_series3']
ts = np.random.rand(6,3)


df_to_extract = pd.DataFrame(data=ts, columns = column_names)

df_to_extract['id'] = 1
df_to_extract['time'] = np.arange(1,7)

#print(df_to_extract)

import tsfresh
from tsfresh import extract_features
from tsfresh import select_features
from tsfresh.utilities.dataframe_functions import impute

from tsfresh.feature_extraction import FeatureExtractionSettings
from tsfresh.feature_extraction import extract_features
from tsfresh.feature_extraction import extract_features, MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings


extracted_features = extract_features(df_to_extract, 
                                      column_id="id", 
                                      column_sort="time", 
                                      parallelization= 'per_kind', 
                                      feature_extraction_settings= MinimalFeatureExtractionSettings)
```

I'm using Python 3.5 (Anaconda) on Win10.

Any ideas how to solve that issue?

issue_title: Timing Leaks in authenticate()
issue_body:
[Example snippet](https://github.com/caffeinehit/django-oauth2-provider/blob/6b5bc0d3ad706d2aaa47fa476f38406cddd01236/provider/oauth2/backends.py#L73-L82)

It appears that OAuth2 backends are using database lookups to validate access tokens. While relational databases are great for performant lookups, they're not so great for comparing cryptographic secrets without leaking timing information. To wit:

* [http://blog.astrumfutura.com/2010/10/nanosecond-scale-remote-timing-attacks-on-php-applications-time-to-take-them-seriously/](http://blog.astrumfutura.com/2010/10/nanosecond-scale-remote-timing-attacks-on-php-applications-time-to-take-them-seriously/)
* [http://www.computerworld.com/article/2519195/security0/researchers--authentication-crack-could-affect-millions.html](http://www.computerworld.com/article/2519195/security0/researchers--authentication-crack-could-affect-millions.html)
* [http://codahale.com/a-lesson-in-timing-attacks/](http://codahale.com/a-lesson-in-timing-attacks/)
* [http://matasano.com/research/TimeTrial.pdf](http://matasano.com/research/TimeTrial.pdf)
* [https://github.com/dmayer/time_trial](https://github.com/dmayer/time_trial)

A Python function that addresses this has already been written in the EFF's [OpenWireless project](https://github.com/EFForg/OpenWireless/blob/757b478195236553188b228675c20223ca1f2c86/routerapi/auth.py#L67-L77)

issue_title: rusage.contents could contain multiple equals signs
issue_body:
In helpers.py:
````python

````

It splits the attributes in rusage.contents with:
````python
   k, v = attr.split('=')
````
On my system (PBS Pro 12.2 and pbs-drmma library) there is the possibility of an attribute containing an equals signs so it requires:
````python
    k, v = attr.split('=',1)
````

Otherwise it does not cleanly end the job and causes problems in software such as Galaxy.
Happy to provide patch.

issue_title: Strange intermittent failure in test_nursery_fixture.test_try on MacOS
issue_body:
See here: https://travis-ci.org/python-trio/pytest-trio/jobs/406738301

I don't understand this at all: the test opens a `SocketListener`, then uses `open_stream_to_socket_listener` to connect to it. Somehow the connection attempt times out (at the OS level -- notice that pytest says the test suite took almost 90 seconds). This should not be possible. If the socket is closed, then either it wasn't bound yet, or it was already closed.

It can't not be bound yet, because we have an address to connect to (`('::1', 49734, 0, 0)` in that log), and we get that from the open socket. I guess it could be bound but not listening? But `open_tcp_listeners` always does both before creating a `SocketListener` object, so that doesn't make sense.

Could the socket have been closed already? The test spawns a call to `serve_tcp` in the nursery fixture, and doesn't do anything to cancel it. So it *should* still be running, unless there was some kind of error, or the fixture was cancelled for some reason. But I can't think of any reason it would be cancelled except for an error – we do call `nursery.cancel_scope.cancel()`, but not until after the test function has finished, so that doesn't apply here. And if there were an error, then it should be showing up in the test log!

So... I'm baffled.

It didn't appear when I ran the tests a second time.

This failure is worrisome, because this test does exactly what we recommend users do in order to avoid this kind of failure :-(.

issue_title: ExaBGP fails parsing AS4_PATH attribute on an AS2 session
issue_body:
Hi Thomas,

I came across a weird problem that was causing one of our BGP sessions to continually die and then restart, and flap endlessly.

Situation:
Our ExaBGP advertises AS4 capability. Peer does not. Therefore this should be NEW -> OLD speaker situation (RFC6793).
The peer has received a BGP update from an upstream. Since the peer only supports AS2, the upstream sends the AS4_PATH attribute (optional, transitive) with some AS4s in the path, which our peer then passes on to us.
However, with this particular peer, every time ExaBGP sees a AS4_PATH attribute, it fails with a packet decode error, sends a Notify and tears down the session.

Example debug output:
```
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  wire          session 1 outgoing 2001:7f8:24::fd / 2001:7f8:24::9 RECEIVED  (47) FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF 0076 02

Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  message       Peer  2001:7f8:24::9 ASN 29691   << UPDATE
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  parser        parsing flag 40 type 01 (origin) len 01 payload  (2) 00
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  parser        parsing flag 40 type 02 (as-path) len 0c payload  (29) 0205 73FB 1B1B 329C 28BD 5BA0
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  parser        parsing flag c0 type 11 (as4-path) len 16 payload  (54) 0205 0000 73FB 0000 1B1B 0000 329C 0000 28BD 0004 091E
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  message       Peer  2001:7f8:24::9 ASN 29691   Could not decode message "2"
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  message       Peer  2001:7f8:24::9 ASN 29691   type object 'AS4Path' has no attribute '_AS4Path__new_aspaths'
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  wire          session 1 outgoing 2001:7f8:24::fd / 2001:7f8:24::9 SENDING  (154) FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF FFFF 003E 0301 0063 616E 206E 6F74 2064 6563 6F64 6520 7570 6461 7465 206D 6573 7361 6765 206F 6620 7479 7065 2022 3222
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  message       Peer  2001:7f8:24::9 ASN 29691   >> NOTIFICATION (1,0,"can not decode update message of type "2"")
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  network       Peer  2001:7f8:24::9 ASN 29691   out loop, peer reset, message [notification sent (1,0)] error[Message header error / Unspecific / can not decode update message of type "2"]
Nov 25 15:27:21 rrc20 exabgp 40619  wire          session 1 outgoing, closing connection from 2001:7f8:24::fd to 2001:7f8:24::9
```

P.S. it reliably does this over both IPv4 and IPv6 sessions. And I think we have only seen it on this one peer, because most of our peers are AS4 capable. Also, this is version 3.4.12 with the patch for issue 337 applied.

I've emailed you the PCAP and full debug output for a particular session in case you need more details.

If you need any further info, just let me know.

Thanks!
Colin

issue_title: Incorrect use of `dynamic_range` name
issue_body:
The term `dynamic range` is often improperly used in the field of image processing as an extension to the usage in audio signal processing. This problem also appears in `scikit-image`.

From https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynamic_range the dynamic range of a value is:

```
max(values interval) / min(values interval)
```

while most of the functions in scikit-image actually want:

```
abs(max(values interval) - min(values interval))
```

For sound signals, which are often expressed in decibel, the max - min definition is an acceptable extension (because the logarithm function used to define the decibel maps ratios to differences). For images, and in general for mathematical objects whose values are not defined in logarithmic terms, this nomenclature is less appropriate.

Therefore, I would suggest to change the scikit-image nomenclature from `dynamic_range` to one of: 
- `values_interval_size`: that would be the exact (but long) name
- `values_interval`: shorter; `interval` is often used as a synonym of `interval_size`
- `peak_to_peak`: quite clumsy to me, but consistent with `numpy` (i.e. `numpy.ptp`)

issue_title: Add stemmer generators for C# code
issue_body:
Hi there,

I would like to know if anyone is interested on having support for C# generators in this project. Generating C# code is actually quite different from what the Java generator does, as those languages, albeit similar, don't have the same constructs (i.e. labelled breaks/loops). As such, in order to create a generator for C#, it would be necessary to mix parts from the C/C++ generator and the Java one. Plus, it would also be interesting to have a .NET library for Snowball, in the same spirit as the Java one that is available in this repository.

As such, I just wanted to add that I have started a new fork to address those issues that is almost completely finished, with support for a new [generator_csharp.c](https://github.com/cesarsouza/snowball/blob/csharp-development/compiler/generator_csharp.c) that can already generate all stemmer definition files that come with Snowball. The generated C# files could be seen, for example, here:

 - https://github.com/cesarsouza/snowball/tree/csharp-development/csharp/Snowball/Algorithms

I have also added some unit tests for some languages, though not all of them yet. So, if any project maintainer would like this feature, I can send a pull request to merge my fork'd version. Otherwise I can just keep my fork around for anyone else that could be interested.

The fork is currently available at [https://github.com/cesarsouza/snowball](https://github.com/cesarsouza/snowball). In order to generate C# files, you can pass "-cs" to the command line compiler, as in

 - snowball.exe stem_ISO_8859_1.sbl -cs -o EnglishStemmer -name EnglishStemmer -u   

An [example generated file can be found here](https://github.com/cesarsouza/snowball/blob/csharp-development/csharp/Snowball/Algorithms/EnglishStemmer.generated.cs).

Hope it can be useful! :smiley:

issue_title: Cannot load class with metaclass thats using dynamic classes
issue_body:
These are my first steps with `dill` and I am trying to pickle a calculation result of the `backtrader` framework which is a stock trading framework (https://www.backtrader.com/).

Using `dill` did not work out of the box. It failed on trying to unpickle the object. `backtrader` makes heavy use of metaclasses and classes that are generated at runtime so I guess it is a difficult case for `dill`.

From the `backtrader` framework I extracted and copy/pasted some code to build a minimal example to show the problem. It fails on `dill.load(open(filename, 'rb'))`:
```python
import sys
import dill
from collections import OrderedDict


class MetaParams(type):
    def __new__(meta, name, bases, dct):
        # Remove params from class definition to avod inheritance
        # (and hence "repetition")
        newparams = dct.pop('params', ())

        # Create the new class - this pulls predefined "params"
        cls = super(MetaParams, meta).__new__(meta, name, bases, dct)

        # Subclass and store the newly derived params class
        cls.params = MetaParams._derive(name, newparams)
        return cls

    @classmethod
    def _derive(cls, name, info):
        clsinfo = OrderedDict()
        clsinfo.update(info)

        clsmodule = sys.modules['__main__']
        newclsname = "MyDynamicClass"

        newcls = type(newclsname, (cls,), {})
        setattr(newcls, '_getpairs', classmethod(lambda cls: clsinfo.copy()))
        setattr(clsmodule, newclsname, newcls)
        newcls.__module__ = '__main__'

        return newcls


class LineRoot(object, metaclass=MetaParams):
    params = dict(myParam='myValue')


filename = "C:\\archive\\s3.dill"

dill.dump(LineRoot, open(filename, 'wb'))

dill.load(open(filename, 'rb'))
```

This gives me this exception:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2017.3\helpers\pydev\pydevd.py", line 1668, in <module>
    main()
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2017.3\helpers\pydev\pydevd.py", line 1662, in main
    globals = debugger.run(setup['file'], None, None, is_module)
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2017.3\helpers\pydev\pydevd.py", line 1072, in run
    pydev_imports.execfile(file, globals, locals)  # execute the script
  File "C:\Program Files\JetBrains\PyCharm 2017.3\helpers\pydev\_pydev_imps\_pydev_execfile.py", line 18, in execfile
    exec(compile(contents+"\n", file, 'exec'), glob, loc)
  File "C:/Users/vbs/Projects/dilltest/strat.py", line 42, in <module>
    dill.load(open(filename, 'rb'))
  File "C:\Users\vbs\Miniconda3\envs\qrawler\lib\site-packages\dill\dill.py", line 288, in load
    obj = pik.load()
  File "C:\Users\vbs\Miniconda3\envs\qrawler\lib\site-packages\dill\dill.py", line 549, in _create_type
    return typeobj(*args)
  File "C:/Users/vbs/Projects/dilltest/strat.py", line 16, in __new__
    cls.params = MetaParams._derive(name, newparams)
  File "C:/Users/vbs/Projects/dilltest/strat.py", line 22, in _derive
    clsinfo.update(info)
TypeError: 'type' object is not iterable
```

My interpretation of what is happening (while I am sure you are much better at understanding it as I am):
The class `LineRoot` has to be pickled. It gets instantiated by the metaclass `MetaParams`. On normal run, the parameter `dct` in this method:
```python
class MetaParams(type):
    def __new__(meta, name, bases, dct):
```
is a `dict` which contains the entry `params` which again is a `dict`. Based on `params` the method `_derive` creates a new class which is assigned as the new value of the class variable `params`.

Now when running the unpickle process then the `params` entry in the `dct` already is the dynamic class that should be created later. The code does not expect it to be that and fails.

---
In the code there are several places where this pattern is used. Is there any chance to get it working with `dill`?
Big thanks in advance!

issue_title: Switch defaults of planemo test.
issue_body: ``planemo test`` should be roughly the same as ``planemo test --conda_dependency_resolution --conda_auto_install --conda_auto_init`` by default. We've picked a winner from a best-practices stand point at least - and each of those things can be more finely controller by those who need it.

issue_title: Thread Safe I/O Redirection
issue_body: Extend idea started in #368 to have a thread local sys variable that is passed around to everything that produces I/O and is used for printing to a pseudo standard out and err.

issue_title: --keep-unreferenced-defs --enable-id-stripping
issue_body:
Prozessing [File:Oxygen480-mimetypes-hidef-text-x-texinfo.svg](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oxygen480-mimetypes-hidef-text-x-texinfo.svg) with

issue_title: Question about kernel patches, series vs standalone
issue_body:
I'm looking at the patchsets in the script dir and I'm assuming that the series patches 0001, 0002 are meant to be the new means of patching newer kernels instead of the standalone realsense-camera-formats.patch.

However, I noticed that the realsense-camera-formats.patch includes several formats which are not included in the series patches: Y8, Y10, Y12, Y16, RAW8, RAW16.  Y8I, Y12I, and Z16 are all omitted as well, but those have been included in the mainline kernel, so it makes sense that the series patches don't include them.   Are the excluded formats no longer necessary?

Also, would it make sense to add a new patch before the existing 0001 which includes Y8I, Y12I, and Z16 (and increment the existing patches in the series), thus allowing you to drop all patches but the series patches (haven't confirmed, but that's my suspicion)? Only kernel-4.4 would then apply new-0001 and then everyone else would apply new-0002 and new-0003. Would also streamline the process of adding new formats in the future as well as handling new kernels as (presumably) these camera formats get upstreamed

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/ralf/raiden/raiden/messages.py", line 431, in as_bytes
    packed.amount = self.amount
  File "/home/ralf/raiden/raiden/encoding/format.py", line 120, in __setattr__
    raise ValueError('value with length {length} for {attr} is to big'.format(length=length, attr=name))
ValueError: value with length 33 for amount is to big
```

It looks like this is only one of those checks that should use >= instead of > in raiden.messages

issue_title: Use geth's clique PoA in tests/CI
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The PoW based `geth` blockchain backend degraded in speed since we initially implemented it. Not only we are bitten by difficulty adjustment for long tests, but also does `geth` internally delay mining if we progress too fast:
```
INFO [07-24|15:38:44] Mining too far in the future             wait=2s
```

PoW furthermore requires DAG preparation (up to 10 minutes) and storage/caching (>=1GB), memory (>=1GB) and a considerable amount of CPU.

Since we run our tests with only miner node and do not test for consensus edge-cases (as of yet), we can switch to [clique PoA](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/issues/225), which is supported by `geth` and which we also use as backend for the `raiden smoketest` command.

This will minimize the use of resources and will allow for a rather stable block time of (minimally) around 1 second.

## Solution

I started the work on this in a branch at https://github.com/konradkonrad/raiden/tree/tests_with_clique -- initial results are quite promising: the `long_running` test set takes 200 seconds with PoA instead of 420 seconds with PoW on my machine.

What is missing from my branch is the proper encoding of the authorized miner/signer address (and only this one) in the genesis block ([see here](https://github.com/konradkonrad/raiden/commit/c1b69a9a0ef54fb19d1ae5a073c3be51bfa44883#diff-60a9b084203a3ca241410ac196d6065dR245) and [here](https://github.com/konradkonrad/raiden/commit/c1b69a9a0ef54fb19d1ae5a073c3be51bfa44883#diff-282099ef9fa82f2e3cbda8360e9c1689R139) ) for all fixture paths. 

The simplest approach to this could be to use a fixed miner account across all tests and probably remove all code that allows for multi-(geth-)node test setups.
## Tasklist

- [ ] fix the signer address in fixture genesis blocks
- [ ] remove DAG generation and caching from `.travis.yml`
- [ ] assess test run time for all `geth` backed tests (especially for tests that only use a low number of blocks)

issue_title: Request for new maintainer
issue_body:
I picked up the maintainer torch about a year ago when the project seemed to be stuck. I was using DJA on a side project, and I needed to fix a bug and get a new release out. Today, I'm no longer working on the side project and my interest in maintaining DJA has waned significantly.

As I'm not being a great steward for the project, I'd like to request that someone else pick it up. This is very much a community effort and code contributions come from a wide variety of contributors. The new maintainer would be most involved with:
1. Review of new Pull Requests and Issues.
2. Deploying new releases to PyPI.

I hope that I've added the right tools that can make management of this project easier for a future maintainer. Static code analysis has removed a lot of friction that might come up as a primary PR reviewer.

If you're in a decent place to step into the maintainer role, please let me know and I can work to transition over project handling to you. I'm happy to help with a bit of training if needed or improving release documentation.

issue_title: [checkoutlinesufo] consider incoming UFO type
issue_body:
As indicated in my UFO3 issue here, checkoutlinesufo follows somewhat of a hybrid approach:
It writes UFO3-style layers into UFO2 files.
If I remember correctly, the UFO version number is manually changed back to 2 after writing the UFO3 layer with defcon (see here: https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/afdko/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/CheckOutlinesUFO.py#L143-L153)

checkoutlinesufo fails when run on a real UFO3 file:
```
Saving font...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/checkoutlinesufo", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/afdko/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/CheckOutlinesUFO.py", line 1146, in main
    run(sys.argv[1:])
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/afdko/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/CheckOutlinesUFO.py", line 1135, in run
    font_file.save()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/afdko/Tools/SharedData/FDKScripts/CheckOutlinesUFO.py", line 139, in save
    writer = UFOWriter(self.defcon_font.path, formatVersion=2)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ufoLib/__init__.py", line 581, in __init__
    raise UFOLibError("The UFO located at this path is a higher version (%d) than the version (%d) that is trying to be written. This is not supported." % (previousFormatVersion, formatVersion))
ufoLib.UFOLibError: The UFO located at this path is a higher version (3) than the version (2) that is trying to be written. This is not supported.
```

It would be great if checkoutlinesufo could recognize the incoming file format, and adjust its behavior accordingly.

issue_title: Interleaving images
issue_body: GalSim's build-in methods to generate surface brightness profiles, for e.g. ```galsim.Gaussian```, ```galsim.OpticalPSF``` etc., can be explicitly convolved with the native pixel response and then drawn using a smaller pixel scale to give images that are finely sampled. However, when pixel level effects, like non-linearity and IPC have to be incorporated, then the images have to be drawn at the native pixel scale, which are typically sampled below Nyquist rate. Highly sampled images including these pixel level effects can be obtained from a dither sequence with uniform offsets by interleaving them, without having to invoke external image combination softwares.

issue_title: what is the correct way to do with forwarded domains
issue_body:
We're using a domain-forwarded setup in which the code itself runs on `project-plus-random-prefix.ourstagingserver.org` but this code is accessed through `project-staging.ourserver.org` (which acts as router for a number of different requests, one set of those being django routes, others being completely different things). When using Google Auth (oauth2) we can't seem to point the google callback to `project-staging.ourserver.org/soc/complete/google-oauth2` because the redirect_url that the social auth library adds into the login is for  `project-plus-random-prefix.ourstagingserver.org`, and I cannot seem to find anything in the docs that explain how to tell social auth what the actual redirect uri should be.

I did find `SOCIAL_AUTH_LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL` in http://python-social-auth-docs.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration/settings.html#urls-options but this documentation seems to suggest this is a *path* rather than a full URL, so if this value can be used for a fully qualified URL, can the docs be updated to explicitly mention that? And if not, what is the correct way to make sure social-auth uses the domain that it needs to be using for auth to succeed?

issue_title: Spanner ignores provided credentials when executing a query.
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
(This bug is re-posted from an e-mail I received yesterday.)

----------------------

We are using python spanner client libraries, and I have a credentials issue in executing a sql query. Seems like executing a sql ignores the provided credentials to the client and just try to use the default credentials.

The code looks like:

```python
spanner_client = spanner.Client(credentials=credentials, project=project_id)
instance = spanner_client.instance(instance_id)
database = instance.database(database_id)
database.execute_sql(stmt)
```

Debugging the code for a bit, I found out that eventually the code [will try to create a session](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/spanner/google/cloud/spanner/session.py#L97) and it will [try to get the spanner API client](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/spanner/google/cloud//spanner/database.py#L157). When this object created, credentials is not passed and hence it's `None`, and hence the SpannerClient is trying to get the default credentials and fails in my case because I do not have default creds.
